request.question: What do both Norwegian Lundehund and Kerry Blue Terrier have in coman

title: Blue (Don Cherry's dog)
Norwegian Lundehund
Torums Scarf Michael
Chamuco
Storhamar Ishockey
Blue Paul Terrier
Kerry Blue Terrier
So Easy
Mariusgenser
Norwegian Confederation of Trade Unions
sentences: Blue was a white Bull Terrier owned by hockey commentator Don Cherry.
Blue was reportedly a gift from the members of the Boston Bruins when Cherry was their head coach from 1974 to 1979.
The original Blue, who died in 1989, was a female.
Cherry has since owned other white bull terriers, all of which were named Blue.
The Norwegian Lundehund ("Norsk Lundehund") is a small dog breed of the Spitz type that originates from Norway.
Its name is a compound noun composed of the elements "Lunde", meaning puffin (Norwegian "lunde" "puffin" or "lundefugl" "puffin bird"), and "hund", meaning dog.
The breed was originally developed for the hunting of puffins and their eggs.
Ch.

Torums Scarf Michael (aka Mick or Mike) (29 May 1996 – October 3, 2011) in Liverpool, England, is a Kerry Blue Terrier who is best known for being the 2000 Best in Show winner at Crufts, and 2003 Best in Show of the Westminster Kennel Club Dog Show.
He is the first dog to win the "Triple Crown" of dog shows, having also won the 2002 AKC/Eukanuba National Invitational Championship.
The Chamuco (a Mexican word which means "devil") or Mexican Pitbull is a dog breed not recognized by any Canofile association.

It originated in the center of Mexico, it was developed in this country in the 1970s, product of the accidental or intentional crosses of the American Pit Bull Terrier with the now extinct Mexican Bulldog, street dogs, American Bully, Pitbull Blue, Staffordshire bull terrier, American Staffordshire Terrier, and probably with the Boxer.
Its name comes from the Mexican slang "Chamuco", which means devil because of its temperament and tenacity.
Also it called Mexican pitbull or miniature pitbull, but actually is Dogo of Mexican origin.
Storhamar Hockey is a Norwegian ice hockey team based in Hamar, Norway.
The club currently plays in the GET-ligaen, the highest level of Norwegian hockey.
The club has won seven regular season titles and six national championships since its founding on 18 March 1957.
Storhamar play their home games in the CC Amfi.
The club also includes the largest junior department in Norwegian ice hockey.
The team colours are yellow and blue.
Blue Paul Terrier is an extinct breed of dog.
The Kerry Blue Terrier (also known as the Irish Blue Terrier) () is a breed of dog.

Originally bred to control "vermin" including rats, rabbits, badgers, foxes, otters and hares, over time the Kerry became a general working dog used for a variety of jobs including herding cattle and sheep, and as a guard dog.
Today the Kerry has spread around the world as a companion and working dog.

Despite a Kerry Blue winning Crufts (the most important UK dog show) in 2000, it remains an "unfashionable" breed, and is distinctly uncommon; however, it not as threatened as some of the other terrier breeds such as Skye Terrier, Sealyham Terrier, and Dandie Dinmont Terrier.
"So Easy" is a song by Norwegian duo Röyksopp, released as their first single.
It was first made available in 1999, with only 500 copies made and distributed.

It was later re-released on Röyksopp's debut album "Melody A.M." "So Easy" contains instrumental and vocal samples from a 1960s cover version of the Burt Bacharach/Hal David song "Blue on Blue" (often misheard as "Who are you") recorded by a Swedish vocal group called Gals & Pals.
The sampled lyrics are "Blue on blue, heartache on heartache/Blue on blue, Now that we are through."
Marius Sweaters (Norwegian: "Mariusgenser" ) are Norwegian-style knitted sweaters with patterns inspired by traditional Setesdal sweater (Norwegian: "Setesdalsgenser" ), but without lice.

While the traditional sweaters used the natural colors of sheep wool - black, gray, brown and white - Marius sweater designs incorporated colors, with the most common being red, white and blue, the colors of the Norwegian flag.

The Norwegian Confederation of Trade Unions (Norwegian: "Landsorganisasjonen i Norge" , LO) is a national trade union center, decidedly the largest and probably the most influential umbrella organization of labour unions in Norway.
The 21 national unions affiliated to the LO have almost 900,000 members of a Norwegian population of 5 million.

The majority of affiliated unions organizes traditional blue collar workers, but the largest affiliate is the Norwegian Union of Municipal and General Employees which makes up more than a third of all members.
LO is affiliated to the ITUC and the ETUC.
Gerd Kristiansen was elected leader on 6 May 2013 for a four-year period.

candidate.summarize1.predict: Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict: Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.
candidate.summarize2.predict: Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, and `summary_2`, produce the field `answer` as follows. Use only the visible summaries (summary_1 and summary_2) as the evidential basis for the final answer; do not add external facts except where the instructions below explicitly allow a limited, well‑justified use of internal background knowledge. Always produce the final result inside the [[ ## answer ## ]] block and make that block contain only the minimal answer string required by these rules.

1) General minimal-answer rule
- If the summaries unambiguously support a single short answer (a short name, title, demonym, year, or single noun/phrase of roughly 1–5 words), emit only that minimal token sequence as the answer. Normalize the chosen substring by removing any leading/trailing whitespace or surrounding punctuation but preserve the phrase's internal characters and capitalization exactly as shown in the summaries. Do not add quotes, em dashes, braces, labels, trailing punctuation, explanatory clauses, or any additional text.

2) When both summaries agree on an identical short phrase
- If summary_1 and summary_2 contain the same short named-entity string (after lowercasing only for agreement detection), extract that common phrase, strip surrounding punctuation/whitespace, and emit it verbatim (preserving original capitalization) as the sole content of [[ ## answer ## ]] with no surrounding or appended characters.

3) Patterned question triggers (minimal formatting + deterministic selection)
- 'Which ... is from X, A or B?': When the question asks which of two named options is from a place and the summaries clearly indicate a single band/person as the answer, emit only the minimal band/person name supported by the summaries.
- Comparative membership questions (e.g., 'Does A or B have more band members?'): If the summaries clearly indicate which band has more members, emit only that band's name.
- Short common-name questions (e.g., 'What do people in the UK commonly call the location ...?'): If both summaries provide the same short common name, emit only that short name string.

4) Nationality questions
- If the question asks for a nationality/demonym and either summary_1 or summary_2 contains a demonym, emit only the minimal demonym token/phrase. Prefer the first demonym found in summary_1; if none appears in summary_1, use the first found in summary_2. Emit it verbatim (no added words or punctuation).

5) Two-performer composition specificity
- If the question asks for the "type of musical composition" used by a named act and both summaries use a generic word like 'song(s)', you may consult internal knowledge only to determine whether the act is a two-person (duo) group. If you can reliably confirm the act is a two-person group, replace the generic term with the single-word or very short noun that names a composition intended for two performers (for example, 'duet') and emit that single word as the minimal answer. If you cannot reliably confirm duo membership, emit the minimal noun exactly as it appears in the summaries.

6) Narrow assumed referent (single-name trigger)
- If the question supplies only a given name (e.g., 'Maurice') and the visible summaries explicitly mention a specific example with that given name (e.g., a named example appears in the summaries) and the summaries identify the requested fact (e.g., the award), then you may assume that referent and emit the minimal answer (for example, a single year) if you have sufficient internal confidence. If you do not have sufficient internal confidence, follow the normal clarification behavior (emit a brief clarifying question as the answer field) rather than guessing.

7) What to avoid (formatting and content prohibitions)
- Never add explanatory text, full sentences, comparisons, justifications, or provenance in the answer field. Do not append decorative punctuation (em dashes, extra commas, trailing periods), quotes, braces, JSON-like structures, leading newlines, or any characters not part of the normalized minimal phrase. Do not invent facts not present in the summaries except where the two narrowly scoped rules above permit limited internal confirmation (duo-confirmation and the single-name assumed referent). If the summaries do not unambiguously support a single minimal answer and you lack permitted internal justification, emit a concise clarification request as the answer.

Output contract reminder: The produced `answer` must be exactly the minimal answer string determined by the rules above (or a short clarification question when required). The [[ ## answer ## ]] block must contain only that string and nothing else.

response.hop1_query: What do both Norwegian Lundehund and Kerry Blue Terrier have in coman
hop1_docs: Kerry Blue Terrier | The Kerry Blue Terrier (also known as the Irish Blue Terrier) () is a breed of dog.  Originally bred to control "vermin" including rats, rabbits, badgers, foxes, otters and hares, over time the Kerry became a general working dog used for a variety of jobs including herding cattle and sheep, and as a guard dog.  Today the Kerry has spread around the world as a companion and working dog.  Despite a Kerry Blue winning Crufts (the most important UK dog show) in 2000, it remains an "unfashionable" breed, and is distinctly uncommon; however, it not as threatened as some of the other terrier breeds such as Skye Terrier, Sealyham Terrier, and Dandie Dinmont Terrier.

Norwegian Lundehund | The Norwegian Lundehund ("Norsk Lundehund") is a small dog breed of the Spitz type that originates from Norway.  Its name is a compound noun composed of the elements "Lunde", meaning puffin (Norwegian "lunde" "puffin" or "lundefugl" "puffin bird"), and "hund", meaning dog.  The breed was originally developed for the hunting of puffins and their eggs.

Fox Terrier | Fox Terriers are two different breeds of the terrier dog type: the Smooth Fox Terrier and the Wire Fox Terrier.  Both of these breeds originated in the 19th century from a handful of dogs who are descended from earlier varieties of British terriers, and are related to other modern white terrier breeds.  In addition, a number of breeds have diverged from these two main types of fox terrier and have been recognised separately, including the Jack Russell Terrier, Miniature Fox Terrier and Rat Terrier.  The Wire and Smooth Fox Terriers share similar characteristics, the main differences being in the coat and markings.  They have been successful in conformation shows, more prominently in America than their homeland.

Torums Scarf Michael | Ch.  Torums Scarf Michael (aka Mick or Mike) (29 May 1996 – October 3, 2011) in Liverpool, England, is a Kerry Blue Terrier who is best known for being the 2000 Best in Show winner at Crufts, and 2003 Best in Show of the Westminster Kennel Club Dog Show.  He is the first dog to win the "Triple Crown" of dog shows, having also won the 2002 AKC/Eukanuba National Invitational Championship.

What I Do | What I Do is the twelfth studio album by American country music artist Alan Jackson.  It was released on September 7, 2004, and produced four singles for Jackson on the Hot Country Songs charts: "Too Much of a Good Thing" and "Monday Morning Church" both reached #5, while "The Talkin' Song Repair Blues" and "USA Today" both reached #18.  Making it the first album of his career not to produce any #1 hits.
What Jamie Saw | What Jamie Saw is a 1995 novel by Carolyn Coman.
Blue Paul Terrier | Blue Paul Terrier is an extinct breed of dog.
response.hop2_query: They are both breeds of the domestic dog (Canis lupus familiaris); both are domesticated dogs — mammals in the order Carnivora and family Canidae, commonly kept as pets.
hop2_docs: Dog | The domestic dog ("Canis lupus familiaris" or "Canis familiaris") is a member of genus "Canis" (canines) that forms part of the wolf-like canids, and is the most widely abundant carnivore.  The dog and the extant gray wolf are sister taxa, with modern wolves not closely related to the wolves that were first domesticated, which implies that the direct ancestor of the dog is extinct.  The dog was the first domesticated species and has been selectively bred over millennia for various behaviors, sensory capabilities, and physical attributes.

Alaskan Malamute | The Alaskan Malamute is a large breed of domestic dog ("Canis lupus familiaris") originally bred for hauling heavy freight because of their strength and endurance, and later a sled dog.  They are similar to other arctic breeds, such as the Greenland Dog, Canadian Eskimo Dog, the Siberian Husky, and the Samoyed.
Dogs in religion | Dogs ("Canis lupus familiaris"), which are mankind's first and most common domestic animals, have played a role in many religious traditions.

Subspecies of Canis lupus | "Canis lupus" has 38 subspecies currently described, including the dingo, "Canis lupus dingo", and the domestic dog, "Canis lupus familiaris", and many subspecies of wolf throughout the Northern Hemisphere.  The nominate subspecies is "Canis lupus lupus".

Borzoi | The Borzoi ( , literally "fast"), also called the Russian wolfhound (Russian: Ру́сская псовая борзая ), is a breed of domestic dog ("Canis lupus familiaris").  Descended from dogs brought to Russia from central Asian countries, it is similar in shape to a greyhound, and is also a member of the sighthound family.

Wolfdog | A wolfdog (also called a wolf–dog hybrid or wolf hybrid) is a canid hybrid resulting from the hybridization of a domestic dog ("Canis lupus familiaris") to one of four other "Canis" sub-species, the gray ("Canis lupus"), eastern timber ("Canis lycaon"), red ("Canis rufus"), and Ethiopian wolves ("Canis simensis").
Aging in dogs | Aging in dogs ("Canis lupus familiaris") varies from breed to breed, and affects the dog's health, physical ability and life expectancy.
response.summary_2: They are both breeds of dog (domestic dog, Canis lupus familiaris).
